coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence paper-based sensor for visual detection of carbamate pesticides in food based on CdTe quantum dot and nano ZnTPyP
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hengye
Hu, Ou
Fan, Yao
Xu, Lu
Zhang, Lei
Lan, Wei
Hu, Ying
Xie, Xingrui
Ma, Lixia
She, Yuanbin
Fu, Haiyan
coredata.dc:description: The needing of rapid and sensitive detection method for pesticides is increasing, to facilitate its detection without complicated instruments. Herein, a novel paper-based senor was developed for the visual detection of three carbamate pesticides (metolcarb, carbofuran, and carbaryl) based on CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and nano zinc 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra(4-pyridyl)-21H-23H-porphine (nano ZnTPyP) with a “turn-off-on” mode. This fluorescence sensing model could be applied in the highly selective and sensitive detection of carbamate pesticides both by fluorescence spectrometry or paper-based sensors. Based on the extracted RGB color values of paper, the partial least squares regression (PLSR) was used to accurately quantify the concentrations of carbamate pesticides in different food matrices (apple, cabbage and tea water). This method featured in high speed, low price and high accuracy, and provided a new strategy for the detection of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paper-based sensor
Carbamate pesticides
Quantum dots
Nano porphyrin
Chemometrics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of nano ZnTPyP
Synthesis and characterization of CdTe quantum dots
Preparation of paper-based sensor for visual detection of carbamate pesticides
Detection of carbamate pesticides in real sample
Quenching process of CdTe QDs fluorescence after the addition of nano ZnTPyP
Recovery process of CdTe QDs fluorescence after the addition of carbamate pesticides
Paper-based visual detection for carbamate pesticides

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV–vis spectra of ZnTPyP and nano ZnTPyP; (B) TEM image of nano ZnTPyP; (C) Fluorescence quenching behavior of CdTe (2.9 × 10−3 µmol/L) after the addition of nano ZnTPyP (3.75 µmol/L), ZnTPyP (4.2 µM) and CTAB (5 × 103 µmol/L); (D) The optimizing of pH value. Fluorescence recovery behavior of CdTe (2.9 × 10−3 µmol/L) and nano ZnTPyP (3.75 µmol/L) system in the present of Metolcarb (10 µg/L) with different pH buffer (pH = 6.0, 6.57.0, 7.5, 8.0, 8.5 or 9.0); (E) The fluorescence quenching of CdTe (2.9 × 10−3 µmol/L) with addition of various concentrations of nano ZnTPyP in pH = 7.0 Tris-HCl buffer. Inset: the linear plot between F0/F1 and the concentration of nano ZnTPyP (0–4.5 µmol/L).

(A) The optimizing of the addition amount of nano ZnTPyP during recovery of QDs fluorescence after the addition of Metolcarb. Fluorescence restoring behavior of CdTe (2.9 × 10−3 µmol/L, a) and nano ZnTPyP (3.75 µmol/L, b) system in the presence of Metolcarb (40 µg/L, c). Inset: fluorescence restoring behavior of CdTe (2.9 × 10−3 µmol/L) after the addition of different concentrations of nano ZnTPyP (2.63–4.88 µmol/L) and the corresponding concentrations of Metolcarb fixed at 10 µg/L; (B) The fluorescence intensity of CdTe with addition of Metolcarb, Carbofuran and Carbaryl (40 µg/L); (C) Recovery of CdTe-nano ZnTPyP fluorescence after the addition of Metolcarb. Fluorescence turn on behavior of CdTe (2.9 × 10−3 µmol/L, j) and nano ZnTPyP (3.75 µmol/L, a) system after the addition of different concentrations of Metolcarb with the range of 5–45 µg/L (b-j). Inset: the linear plot between F0/F1 and the concentration of Metolcarb (5–45 µg/L); (D) F2/F0 values of CdTe–nano ZnTPyP with addition of various pesticide residues (Blank, Acetochlor, DM, Cartap, Dipterex, Demeton, Dimethoate, 50 µg/L; Metolcarb, 45 µg/L).
Recovery of CdTe–nano ZnTPyP fluorescence after the addition of carbofuran (A) and carbaryl (B). Inset: the linear plot between F0/F1 and the concentration of carbofuran and carbaryl.
Assigned plots of dummy codes of the training set and prediction set for raw fluorescence spectra in PLSDA model.

(A) The paper-based images of the fluorescence restoring of CdTe-nano ZnTPyP upon addition of various concentrations of Metolcarb (CCdTe = 25.08 µmol/L; Cnano ZnTPyP = 17.85 µmol/L). All the images were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp; (B) The paper-based images of the selectivity of the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP sensor to various pesticides (CCdTe = 25.08 µmol/L; Cnano ZnTPyP = 17.85 µmol/L; Call pesticides = 5 µg/L). All the images were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.
Detection of Metolcarb in apple, cabbage and tea.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence paper-based sensor for visual detection of carbamate pesticides in food based on CdTe quantum dot and nano ZnTPyP

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, Guizhou, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Hengye Chen and Ou Hu equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The needing of rapid and sensitive detection method for pesticides is increasing, to facilitate its detection without complicated instruments. Herein, a novel paper-based senor was developed for the visual detection of three carbamate pesticides (metolcarb, carbofuran, and carbaryl) based on CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and nano zinc 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra(4-pyridyl)-21H-23H-porphine (nano ZnTPyP) with a “turn-off-on” mode. This fluorescence sensing model could be applied in the highly selective and sensitive detection of carbamate pesticides both by fluorescence spectrometry or paper-based sensors. Based on the extracted RGB color values of paper, the partial least squares regression (PLSR) was used to accurately quantify the concentrations of carbamate pesticides in different food matrices (apple, cabbage and tea water). This method featured in high speed, low price and high accuracy, and provided a new strategy for the detection of food safety.

ce:keyword.ce:text: root mean squared error of the calibration
root mean squared error of the prediction

ce:section.ce:para: China is a major agricultural country, but also a major manufacturer of pesticides. Carbamate pesticides, as kinds of insecticides and herbicides, are extensively used to eradicate agricultural pests to increase crop yields. The residues of carbamate pesticides have been frequently found in food and environmental stuff due to their widespread use (Berman et al., 2017). These residues can enter the human body through inhalation, intake and in contact with skin, leading to a series of serious harm such as headache, memory loss, proximal muscle weakness and anorexia etc. (Sahoo et al., 2018). Therefore, there is an increasingly urgent need to develop rapid and sensitive analytical methods for the detection of low concentration of carbamate pesticides in the daily-consumed food and drinking water.

In recent years, numerous efforts have been taken to develop effective approaches for the determination of pesticides in food or drinking water. Liquid/gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (da Costa Morais, Collins, & Jardim, 2018; Lehmann, Oltramare, & De Alencastro, 2018), electrochemical analysis (Uniyal & Sharma, 2018) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Piao et al., 2009) are well-developed for the detection of pesticides. Although these approaches prove to be highly selective and sensitive and allow for the detection of pesticides in complex samples, they involve expensive instruments, complex sample pretreatment and the need for skilled operators. Therefore, the widespread use of these traditional methods has been limited. The emerging chemical and biological sensing methods have been successfully applied to the detection of pesticides due to their high sensitivity and short analysis time (Supharoek, Ponhong, Siriangkhawut, & Grudpan, 2019). Among them, nanomaterials-based chemical and biological sensing methods exhibit great potential for pesticide detection due to the unique chemical or physical properties of nanomaterial (Lin et al., 2018). For example, Zheng et al. integrated acetylcholinesterase enzyme into the optical transducer of CdTe semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) through layer-by-layer assembly technique, which realized high sensitivity detection of organophosphorus pesticides in fruits and vegetables (Zheng, Zhou, Li, Liu, & Tang, 2011). Liu et al. found that the synthesis of rhodamine B-covered gold nanoparticle could be used to detect organophosphorus and carbamate pesticides in complex solutions sensitively (Liu et al., 2012). Additionally, a colorimetric method based on Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticle peroxidase mimetic was successfully designed for rapid detection of organophosphorus pesticides (Liang et al., 2012). All these methods are with the advantages of rapidity and sensitivity compared to large analytical instruments. However, most of the current methods focus on the detection of organophosphorus pesticides (Ahmad et al., 2019); the researches on the detection of carbamate pesticides are rare.

Quantum dots, as semiconductor nanoparticles, are drawn the increasing attention due to their unique optical and electronic properties such as a wide range of excitation spectra, symmetrical and narrow tunable emission spectra, and good light stability. QDs are more promising as fluorescence probes and has been successfully used to detect amino acids (Fu et al., 2019), glucose (Ahmed, Cirone, & Chen, 2019), DNA (Fan et al., 2019), heavy metals (Chen et al., 2017) and so on. In addition, nano porphyrins have good application in various fields such as photosensitive materials (Chen, Li, Huang, Wang, & Kang, 2016), drug development (Li et al., 2014), clinical cancer treatment (Lin et al., 2016) and analytical detection (Yan et al., 2018) due to their unique structural and physicochemical properties. Therefore, the simultaneous use of quantum dots and nano porphyrins exhibits great potential in the field of substance detection.

In this work, we developed a fluorescent paper-based sensor method for the highly sensitive detection of carbamate pesticides based on CdTe QDs and nano zinc 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-pyridyl)- 21H-23H-porphyrin (nano ZnTPyP) for the first time. The nano ZnTPyP was synthesized by cationic surfactant-assisted method and used as fluorescence quencher. The negatively charged carbamate pesticide could react with the positively charged nano ZnTPyP through electrostatic interaction, which pushed the nano ZnTPyP leave the surface of QDs and restored the fluorescence of QDs. The limit of detection (LOD) for carbamate pesticides of our method was significantly comparable with the traditional chromatographic techniques. More importantly, this fluorescence sensing based on “on-off-on” model could be further applied to paper chips, realizing rapid, sensitive, economical and instrument-free visual detection of carbamate pesticides. Furthermore, the results indicated that the proposed fluorescent paper-based sensor could be applied to the detection of carbamate pesticides in complex vegetables, fruits and tea samples.

Apple and Chinese cabbage (cabbage) were bought in local supermarkets (Wuhan, China). Long Jing green tea (tea) was provided by Hangzhou Tea Research Institute of All-China Federation of Supply and Marketing Co-operatives (Hangzhou, China). Cadmium chloride (CdCl2), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), acetone, hydrochloric acid and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 3-hydroxymethyl-methane (Tris) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3) and N-acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Metolcarb, carbofuran, carbaryl, acetochlor, deltamethyrin (DM), cartap, dipterex, demeton, dimethoate, dichlorvos (2,2-dichlorovinyldimethyl phosphate, DDVP) were all purchased from Shanghai pesticide research institute Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra(4-pyridyl)-21H-23H-porphine (TPyP) were prepared by the reported method (Kitaoka, Nobuoka, Ihara, & Ishikawa, 2014). Quantitative filter papers were obtained from Hangzhou Wohua Filter Paper Co., Ltd (Zhejiang, China). All solutions were prepared from fresh deionized distilled water. UV–vis spectra were recorded by using a UV–vis Spectrophotometer (UV-1800 PC) with the baseline correction. The fluorescence spectra were acquired by scanning with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Tech Company, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by a Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700-FT-IR spectrometer (USA). The morphology and particle size of the quantum dot CdTe were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN, USA). The circular paper sheets were obtained by the cutting of a laser engraving machine (Shandong Xinbang Co., Ltd., China). All images on paper were taken by an iPhone6 camera. The obtained photos were simulated by Photoshop software to extract RGB values. Both partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLSDA) and partial least squares regression (PLSR) methods were performed by Matlab software (MathWorks, USA) for data processing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of nano ZnTPyP
ce:section.ce:para: Nano ZnTPyP was synthesized by the surfactant-assisted method (Qiu, Chen, & Liu, 2010). 0.0037 g of ZnTPyP powder was dissolved in 5 mL of tetrahydrofuran solution, and 2.8 mL of the solution was taken up with 40 mL of CTAB (5 × 103 µmol/L) aqueous solution, then stirred at room temperature for half an hour. Finally, nano ZnTPyP were successfully prepared by the obtainment of a transparent ondine solution. UV–vis spectra of the solution were measured and shown in the Fig. 1 A. Nano ZnTPyP had an ultraviolet absorption peak around 460 nm, implying the formation of J-type aggregation. The characterization of TEM was shown in Fig. 1B, showing that nanorods with a diameter of 500 nm were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CdTe quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs were synthesized based on the reported method with some modification (Yan et al., 2018). 0.1096 g of CdCl2 and 0.1567 g (15 × 103 µmol/L) of NAC were added to 40 mL of ultrapure water, stirring at room temperature for 15 min. Then the pH of the solution was adjusted to 4.60 with sodium hydroxide solution. Afterwards, the solution was stirred under a nitrogen atmosphere in an ice bate for 20 min. 0.0532 g of Na2TeO3 and 0.0109 g of NaBH4 were added into the solution and stirred for 15 min. Finally, the solution was heated at 200 °C for 50 min. Green-emission NAC-modified CdTe fluorescent probe (λem = 550 nm) was obtained, when it cooled down to room temperature. The concentration of NAC-modified CdTe QDs was calculated as 2.51 × 101 µmol/L by its UV–vis absorbance spectra (Yu, Qu, Guo, & Peng, 2003).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper-based sensor for visual detection of carbamate pesticides
ce:section.ce:para: The paper-based analytical devices were constructed through immobilization of prepared NAC-capped CdTe solutions on paper. Firstly, the quantitative filter paper was cut into several circular paper sheets with a diameter of 6.0 mm by a laser engraving machine. And then, these circular paper sheets were neatly separated on the hydrophobic double faced adhesive tape, which ensured each circular paper sheet being non-interfering and independent. Afterwards, as-prepared 10 μL of NAC-capped CdTe solutions were added on the circular paper. Each circular paper was slightly dried in an oven at 37 °C for 5–7 min. Finally, the 10 μL of the mixture of different concentrations of carbamate pesticides and nano ZnTPyP were dropped on to the circular papers and reacted at room temperature for 5 min. The color responses of the circular papers were observed by the naked eyes under a 365 nm ultraviolet analyzer and taken pictures by the camera of smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of carbamate pesticides in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: Three common foods apple, cabbage and tea, which are susceptible to the contamination of carbamate pesticides, were used to verify the accuracy of the method during the complex food matrices. Apple and cabbage were juiced by a juicer (Joyoung JYL-C50T, China), and the extract of tea wasobtained by soaking 0.08 g of tea in 9 mL 80 °C water for 20 min. Then, the pesticides in different concentrations were added to the juice and extract for testing, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching process of CdTe QDs fluorescence after the addition of nano ZnTPyP
ce:section.ce:para: Porphyrins are good fluorescent quenchers, and nanoporphyrins can quench fluorescence more efficiently, thereby saving the amount of porphyrin used. Therefore, we first explored the effectiveness of the nano ZnTPyP quenching CdTe fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 1C, the CTAB added alone had no quenching effect on the CdTe fluorescent probe, while the addition of the same concentration of ZnTPyP and nano ZnTPyP both caused fluorescence quenching of CdTe fluorescent probe. However, the fluorescence quenching ability of nano ZnTPyP was obviously stronger than that of ZnTPyP. These results demonstrated that the quenching effect of nano ZnTPyP on CdTe QDs was derived from the nano-performance of nano ZnTPyP itself, and the fluorescence quenching ability of nano porphyrin was better. Hence, nano ZnTPyP was determined to be the fluorescent quencher for CdTe fluorescent probes. Subsequently, we optimized the pH of the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system for the detection of carbamate pesticides. In Tris-HCl buffer solution of different pH (6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, 8.5, 9.0), the fluorescence recovery of CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system after the addition of 10 μg/L metolcarb were shown in Fig. 1D. Herein, F0 represented the original fluorescence intensity of CdTe; F1 stood for the fluorescence intensity in the presence of nano ZnTPyP; F2 equaled the fluorescence intensity that recoveried by the carbamate pesticide; and (F2-F1)/F0 represented the fluorescence recovery rate of CdTe. The results of Fig. 1D reflexed that the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system was most beneficial for the detection of carbamate pesticides in pH = 7.0 buffer. To ensure the sensitivity of the detection of carbamate pesticides, the pH value of the buffer solution was finally chosen to be 7.0.

Further, we investigated the fluorescence quenching sensitivity of nano ZnTPyP to CdTe QDs. The result of Fig. 1E showed that 2.9 × 10−3 μmol/L CdTe QDs adding with different concentrations of nano ZnTPyP could produce different degrees of fluorescence quenching which were proportional to the concentration of nano ZnTPyP in the pH = 7.0 buffer system. Moreover, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs and the concentration of nano ZnTPyP conformed to the “Stern-Volmer” equation: F0/F1 = 1 + Ksv[M]. Among them, F0 and F1 represented the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs without and with the addition of nano ZnTPyP, respectively, [M] meant the concentration of nano ZnTPyP, and the quenching constant Ksv was defined as the fluorescence quenching efficiency of nano ZnTPyP. As shown in the inset of Fig. 1E, there was a good linearity between the F0/F1 and the concentration range of nano ZnTPyP from 0 to 4.5 μmol/L. The linear correlation coefficient R was 0.9997. The fluorescence quenching constant Ksv of nano ZnTPyP to QDs was calculated to be 0.44 L/μmol. These results indicated that the quenching sensitivity of nano ZnTPyP towards CdTe QDs was extremely high. Further, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of nano ZnTPyP and the fluorescence emission spectrum of CdTe QDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. S2A, the fluorescence emission spectrum of CdTe QDs almost completely overlapped with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of nano ZnTPyP at the wavelength of 570 nm. Nano ZnTPyP quenched the fluorescence of CdTe QDs by both fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET) based on the reported literature (Fu et al., 2019). The TEM image of CdTe-nano ZnTPyP mixture also demonstrated there was interaction between CdTe QDs and nano ZnTPyP (Fig. S2B), which led to the “turn-off” effect of CdTe QDs fluorescence.

In order to explore the feasibility of detecting carbamate pesticides, metolcarb, as moderate toxic carbamate pesticide, was selected as test object in the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system. The results showed (Fig. 2 A) that the addition of 40 μg/L of metolcarb to the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system could achieve 80.26% fluorescence recovery of its original fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, when 40 μg/L of carbamate pesticides were directly added into CdTe QDs, the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs was almost unaffected (Fig. 2B). These results implied that interaction between nano ZnTPyP and carbamate pesticides results in the fluorescence recovery of CdTe quantum dots. According to the structure characteristics of nano ZnTPyP and carbamate pesticides, the interactions between them were mainly derived from the coordination from Zn2+ and electrophilic interactions from the cation of CTAB. In addition, the steric hindrance of nanostructures of porphyrin also plays a role in the specific identification of carbamate pesticides. And the interaction between nano ZnTPyP and carbamate pesticides is greater than that between nano ZnTPyP and CdTe QDs. The results in Fig. 1E showed that the higher the concentration of nano ZnTPyP increased, the lower the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs decreased. However, the high concentration of nano ZnTPyP was not conducive to the subsequent fluorescence restoring of CdTe QDs. Therefore, the concentration of nano ZnTPyP in CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system needed to be further optimized. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2A, the extremely low or high concentration of nano ZnTPyP did not conducive to the fluorescence restoring of CdTe QDs. The addition of 3.75 µmol/L nano ZnTPyP could cause 37.89% fluorescence quenching of CdTe and its fluorescence recovery rate (F2-F1)/F0 was 0.4233 that was the largest value in all cases. Therefore, the concentration of nano ZnTPyP was chosen to be 3.75 μmol/L in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery process of CdTe QDs fluorescence after the addition of carbamate pesticides
ce:section.ce:para: The CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system for the detection of carbamate pesticides was further explored after optimizing the pH value and the concentration of nano ZnTPyP in CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system. As shown in Fig. 2C, different concentrations (5–45 μg/L) of metolcarb were added to the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system, and the fluorescence emission intensity of CdTe QDs at 552 nm gradually increased. And the F2/F0 value was in a good linear relationship with the concentration of metolcarb from 5 to 45 μg/L (Y = 0.01052X + 0.36618, R = 0.9995), with the LOD value of 0.91 μg/L (LOD = 3 × δ/Slopes, δ: Standard deviation of blank noise) that was lower than the European Union defied maximum residue limit MRL (10 μg/L) of pesticide in food (The Australian Wine Research Institute, 2019). In order to evaluate the selectivity of the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP sensing platform for carbamate pesticides, the fluorescence intensity rations (F2/F0) of CdTe QDs were investigated at the same condition with varieties of pesticides (Fig. 2C). By comparison, the fluorescence intensity ratio had no obvious change in the precence of other prescides (acetochlor, DM, cartap, dipterex, demeton and dimethoate) at 50 μg/L, which manifesting the good selectivity of the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP system to carbamate pesticides over other pesticides.

Furthermore, to investigate the response of the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP sensing platform to other carbamate pesticides, we further studied the high-toxicity carbofuran and low-toxicity carbaryl of carbamate pesticides. As shown in Fig. 3 , the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP sensing platform had gradually fluorescence restoring after the addition of different concentrations of carbofuran and carbaryl. And the F2/F0 value was in a good linear relationship the concentrations of carbofuran and carbaryl in the range of 5–30 μg/L (carbofuran: Y = 0.01082X + 0.46155, R = 0.9989; carbaryl: Y = 0.01232X + 0.43867, R = 0.9992), the LODs were 0.89 μg/L and 0.78 μg/L, respectively. The LODs of three carbamate pesticides of our fluorescence sensor were compared to the reported methods. As shown in Table S1, the LODs of our work were relatively low, and were comparable with chromatographic techniques. The above results demonstrated that the CdTe-nano ZnTPy sensing system could detect carbamate pesticides with high sensitivity and high specificity. In order to further quickly and accurately distinguish different kinds of carbamate pesticides, the PLSDA model was employed to discriminate these three carbamate pesticides based on their fluorescence spectra data. Each pesticide contained 60 samples that were randomly divided into the training set and the prediction set. Details information on the samples was shown in Table S2. The dummy codes for metolcarb, carbofuran and carbaryl samples were defined as f1(1, 0, 0), f2(0, 1, 0), f3(0, 0, 1). All samples were recognized based on the location of the largest dummy code. The number of the latent variables (LVs) was determined as 5 by cross validation. The assigned plots of dummy codes of the training set and prediction of three pesticides were shown in Fig. 4 . The results showed that recognition rate in the training set and prediction set for the three pesticides were 100%, and the sensitivity and specificity of the models were calculated to be 1 (Westerhuis et al., 2008). It was indicated that the CdTe-nano ZnTPy fluorescence sensor combined with the PLSDA model could achieve the simultaneous detection of different carbamate pesticides with high sensitivity and high specificity.

The detection mechanism of the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP fluorescence sensor for carbamate pesticides was shown in Fig. S1. The nano ZnTPyP with the nanorod morpholopy was synthesized by surfactant-assisted method, forming a cationic CTAB-encapsulated nano-micelle system. The nano ZnTPyP quenched the fluorescence of the CdTe QDs by FRET and PET, which produced a “turn-off” effect. However, the amide bond in the added carbamate pesticide was a negatively charged functional group, which had an electrostatic effect with the positively charged cationic CTAB-coated nano ZnTPyP. Since the binding ability of nano ZnTPyP to carbamate pesticides was stronger than that between CdTe, leading to the leaving of nano ZnTPyP from the surface of CdTe QDs and the fluorescence “turn-on” behaviors. The fluorescence “on-off-on” model was utilized to detect carbamate pesticides by the fluorescence intensity changes. This method was faster, easier, more efficient and sensitive than other large analytical instruments, such as high performance liquid chromatography and gas chromatography.

The practicability of the CdTe-nano ZnTPyP for the detection of carbamate pesticides in real samples was further investigated. Different concentrations of metolcarb, carbofuran and carbaryl in different food matrices (apple, cabbage and tea water) were examined by using this method. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of metolcarb were in the range of 92.4%–107.3% for the apple, cabbage and tea water, with relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 6.0% (n = 3). As shown in Table S3 and Table S4, the recoveries of carbofuran and carbaryl in complex matrices were in the range of 95.1%–1036% (RSD ≤ 7.1%) and 95.0%–103.7% (RSD ≤ 7.4%), respectively. These results proved that this CdTe-nano ZnTPyP fluorescence sensor could satisfactorily applied for the detection of carbamate pesticides in complex biological matrices and food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based visual detection for carbamate pesticides
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the widespread use of pesticides, pesticide residues have seriously jeopardized human health and environmental protection. Hence, it is essential to develop a rapid, accurate and instrument-free approach for pesticide. In order to solve this problem, a portable and effective CdTe paper-based sensor for detecting carbamate pesticides was further developed. To make sure of the high sensitivity of paper-based sensor, the concentration of nano ZnTPyP on the paper chip was first optimized. The CdTe-based paper sensor with different concentrations (17.04, 17.85, 18.75, 19.73 and 20.83 μmol/L) of nano ZnTPyP were used to test the same concentration (10 μg/L) of metolcarb. As shown in Fig. S3, the nano ZnTPyP with low concentration was not conducive to the visual detection, while the nano ZnTPyP with high concentrations was also disadvantageous to subsequent fluorescence recovery. As the concentration of nano ZnTPyP was 17.85 μmol/L, its differences of color change between quenching and recovery were most pronounced, which was beneficial for visual detection. Therefore, the concentration of nano ZnTPyP for paper-based sensor was determined to be 17.85 μmol/L. Based on the optimization of the concentration of nano ZnTPyP, the paper chips were further applied to the detection of carbamate pesticides. As shown in Fig. 5 A, different concentrations (1–20 μg/L) of metolcarb were added onto the paper chips. As the concentrations of the metolcarb increased, the changed color of paper was from dark green to yellow green, then to pale green and finally to green, which realized the rapid and visual detection of pesticides. The images of paper were taken by camera under the 365 nm ultraviolet analyzer, extracted and simulated the color RGB values by PS software, showing the same trend of color changes on the paper chips. Furthermore, the carbofuran and carbayl were also visually and quantitatively detected by the paper-based sensor at the same condition (Fig. S4). In order to evaluate the selectivity of paper-based sensor for the detection of carbamate pesticides, various pesticides including acetochlor, DM, cartap, dipterex, DDVP, demeton and dimethoate were thoroughly investigated. Fig. 5B presented that metolcarb could lead to the color change from dark green to yellow green, while other pesticides still induced the color of dark green on the paper chips. It was demonstrated that the development of the novel CdTe-nano ZnTPyP-based paper sensor could successfully achieve the rapid, highly sensitive, highly specific and on-site detection of carbamate pesticides.

To further evaluate the practical utilities of the paper-based sensors for the detection of carbamate pesticides, the developed method was employed to determine carbamate pesticides in complex matrices including apple, cabbage and tea water. These matrices contained different concentrations (1, 5, 10, 15 and 20 μg/L) of metolcarb, carbofuran and carbaryl with standard addition method. It was found that the different concentrations of carbamate pesticides in different complex matrices could still produce different degrees of color change based on the paper-based sensor. Moreover, in order to more rapid and accurate identification of different concentrations of carbamate pesticides in different complex food matrices, partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLSDA) was employed to discriminate different concentrations of carbamate pesticides in different food (apple, cabbage, and tea water) based on the color RGB value data matrix. Each food contained six concentrations (0, 1, 5, 10, 15, and 20 μg/L) of carbamate pesticides, and a total of 60 samples were obtained. Sixty samples were randomly separated into training sets and prediction sets as shown in Table S5. The dummy codes of the six concentrations of carbamate pesticide samples were f01(1,0,0,0,0,0), f02(0,1,0,0,0,0), f03(0,0,1,0,0,0), f04(0,0,0,1,0,0), f05(0,0,0,0,1,0), f06(0,0,0,0,0,1). The pattern recognition was performed according to the location of the largest dummy code. The discrimination results based on PLSDA models were displayed in Table S6. The different concentrations of carbamate pesticides in complex matrices could reach 100% recognition rate both in the training set and prediction set. And their sensitivity and specificity were calculated as 1 (Westerhuis et al., 2008). These results indicated that the different concentrations of carbamate pesticides in complex matrices could be successfully discriminated based on paper-based sensor coupled with the PLSDA model.

More importantly, partial least square regression (PLSR) model were further employed to correlate color RGB values with the concentrations of carbamate pesticides using the extracted features. In order to obtain the best robust and low-complexity model, the latent variables (LVs) were determined to have a low root mean squared error value by eightfold cross validation. The optimal LVs of PLSR models for the carbofuran in different matrices were 6, 6, 7, 6, 8 and 7, and for carbaryl were 12, 8, 8, 7, 8 and 8. The root mean squared error of the calibration (RMSEC) and the root mean squared error of the prediction (RMSEP) in the training set and the prediction set were utilized to estimate the accuracy of the calibration models. The coefficients of determination R 2 c and R 2 p were utilized to evaluate the relevant relationships between the predicted concentration and the actual concentration in the training set and the prediction set, respectively. The prediction results of different models were listed in Table S7. The correlation coefficients in the training set and the prediction set for three pesticides were larger than 0.9990 and the root mean squared error did not exceed 0.1931. The correlation plots between the actual concentration and predicted concentrations of three pesticides in complex matrices were shown in Fig. S5, S7 and S9. The predicted concentration and actual concentration of the three pesticides in different complex matrices were in a good linear relationship and almost had no deviation. To further measure the accuracy of these results, the eilliptical joint confidence regions of paper-based detection for carbamate pesticides were analyzed at 95% confidence and showed in Fig. S10, S6 and, S8. And the ideal values were all within the eilliptical joint confidence regions, which revealed that these paper-based detection results are reliable at 95% confidence (Gonźalez, Herrador & Asuero, 1999). These results manifested that the developed PLSR models were sufficient for the prediction of the concentrations of carbamate pesticide in food samples, and provided an accurate and reliable quantitative result.

A novel CdTe-nano ZnTPyP fluorescent paper-based sensor was developed for the determination of carbamate pesticides. The addition of carbamate pesticides could effectively restore the fluorescence of CdTe QDs quenched by nano ZnTPyP, constructing an “on-off-on” detection model for the carbamate pesticides. More importantly, this method was successfully carried out for the visual detection of carbamate pesticides based on paper chips, which provide a more rapid, inexpensive and effective method for carbamate pesticides. The color of paper chips changed from dark green to yellow green, then to pale green and finally to green upon the addition of carbamate pesticides. And the paper-based senor combined with PLSDA and PLSR models could realize the quantitative and qualitative analysis of carbamate pesticides in complex biological and food matrices with high sensitivity and specificity. The current fluorescence paper-based sensor strategies could be extended to apply such a visual analysis in the biological, chemical, food and agricultural fields.

Hengye Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Ou Hu: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yao Fan: Writing - original draft. Lu Xu: Formal analysis. Lei Zhang: Formal analysis. Wei Lan: Resources. Ying Hu: Resources. Xingrui Xie: Investigation. Lixia Ma: Investigation. Yuanbin She: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Haiyan Fu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

QKHJC[2017]1186, QKHZC[2019]2816, and QKHPTRC[2020]5009), the Talented Researcher Program from Guizhou Provincial Department of Education (QJHKYZ[2018]073), and Tongren science and Technology Bureau (No. TSKY2019-3), the Talented Youth Cultivation Program from “the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities”, and South-Central University for Nationalities (No. CZP20007).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127075.
